---
title: "Replication of Karlan and List (2007)"
subtitle: "Does Price Matter in Charitable Giving? Evidence from a Large-Scale Natural Field Experiment"
author: "Lawrence Lin"
date: today
format: html
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Introduction

Karlan and List (2007) study whether matching grants increase charitable giving. In their field experiment, prior donors were randomly assigned to receive either a standard fundraising letter or a letter offering a matching grant. Because treatment was randomly assigned, differences between the treatment and control groups can be interpreted causally.

In this replication, I do three things. First, I replicate part of Table 1 to check whether treatment and control are balanced on observable characteristics. Second, I replicate the main results from Table 2A, Panel A. Third, I replicate one column of Table 3 using a simple linear regression. As an additional step, I graph response rates by match ratio.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from IPython.display import display

df = pd.read_stata("AERtables1-5.dta")
df.columns = [c.lower() for c in df.columns]

def pick(cands):
    for c in cands:
        if c in df.columns:
            return c
    raise KeyError(f"Missing columns: {cands}")

TREAT = pick(["treatment", "treat", "match"])
GAVE = pick(["gave", "out_gavedum"])
AMOUNT = pick(["amount", "out_amountgive"])

MRM2 = pick(["mrm2"])
HPA = pick(["hpa"])
FREQ = pick(["freq"])
YEARS = pick(["years"])
DORMANT = pick(["dormant", "year5"])

RATIO2 = pick(["ratio2", "treat_ratio2"])
RATIO3 = pick(["ratio3", "treat_ratio3"])

work = df.copy()

for col in [MRM2, HPA, FREQ, YEARS, DORMANT]:
    work[col] = work[col].replace(-999, np.nan)

work[TREAT] = pd.to_numeric(work[TREAT], errors="coerce")
work[GAVE] = pd.to_numeric(work[GAVE], errors="coerce")
work[AMOUNT] = pd.to_numeric(work[AMOUNT], errors="coerce")
```
## Main Analysis

### Replicating part of Table 1

I first replicate part of Table 1 by comparing several pre-treatment characteristics across the treatment and control groups. Because the matching-grant offer was randomly assigned, the two groups should look very similar on observable variables if the randomization worked correctly. This balance check is important because it helps show that later differences in giving are not driven by selection bias.

```{python}
#| echo: false

balance_vars = {
    "Months since last donation": MRM2,
    "Highest previous contribution": HPA,
    "Number of prior donations": FREQ,
    "Years since initial donation": YEARS,
    "Already donated in 2005": DORMANT
}

rows = []
for name, col in balance_vars.items():
    treat = work.loc[work[TREAT] == 1, col].dropna()
    control = work.loc[work[TREAT] == 0, col].dropna()
    _, pval = stats.ttest_ind(treat, control, equal_var=False, nan_policy="omit")
    rows.append({
        "Variable": name,
        "Treatment Mean": treat.mean(),
        "Control Mean": control.mean(),
        "Difference": treat.mean() - control.mean(),
        "p-value": pval
    })

table1_rep = pd.DataFrame(rows)
display(table1_rep.round(3))
```
The treatment and control groups are very similar on these measured characteristics. This is consistent with successful random assignment and suggests that the experiment did not generate meaningful selection bias on observable variables.

### Replicating Table 2A, Panel A

Next, I replicate the main outcomes from Table 2A, Panel A. I report the control group, the overall treatment group, and the three treatment subgroups defined by the match ratio: 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1. I focus on the response rate, average dollars given across all letters, and average dollars given conditional on making a donation.

```{python}
work["_group"] = np.select(
    [
        work[TREAT] == 0,
        (work[TREAT] == 1) & (work[RATIO2] == 0) & (work[RATIO3] == 0),
        (work[TREAT] == 1) & (work[RATIO2] == 1),
        (work[TREAT] == 1) & (work[RATIO3] == 1)
    ],
    ["Control", "1:1", "2:1", "3:1"],
    default="Other"
)

groups = {
    "Control": work.loc[work[TREAT] == 0],
    "Treatment": work.loc[work[TREAT] == 1],
    "1:1": work.loc[work["_group"] == "1:1"],
    "2:1": work.loc[work["_group"] == "2:1"],
    "3:1": work.loc[work["_group"] == "3:1"]
}

rows = []
for g, sub in groups.items():
    rows.append({
        "Group": g,
        "Response Rate": sub[GAVE].mean(),
        "Average Dollars (Unconditional)": sub[AMOUNT].mean(),
        "Average Dollars | Donated": sub.loc[sub[GAVE] == 1, AMOUNT].mean(),
        "N": len(sub)
    })

table2_rep = pd.DataFrame(rows)
display(table2_rep.round(3))
```
## Something Additional

As an additional step, I present the main Table 2A result graphically. The paper reports these response rates in a table, but a figure makes the pattern easier to see. In particular, it helps show that offering any match increases giving relative to the control group, while higher match ratios do not generate a clear additional increase.

```{python}
plot_df = table2_rep.loc[table2_rep["Group"].isin(["Control", "1:1", "2:1", "3:1"])]

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
plt.bar(plot_df["Group"], plot_df["Response Rate"])
plt.xlabel("Group")
plt.ylabel("Response Rate")
plt.title("Response Rate by Match Ratio")
plt.ylim(0, plot_df["Response Rate"].max() * 1.25)
plt.show()
```
